stem: A forest (a graph with no cycles) has \( [[n]] \) vertices and \( [[c]] \) connected components. How many edges does it have?
solution: Each of the \( [[c]] \) components is a tree, and a tree on \( k \) vertices has \( k-1 \) edges. Summing over the components, the total number of edges is \( [[n]] - [[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the whole forest is a single tree (one component), ignoring the \( [[c]] \) components.
This uses the number of vertices, forgetting that a forest has fewer edges than vertices.
This is the number of edges needed to connect the \( [[c]] \) components together, not the edges already present.
This subtracts one edge too many.
This adds one edge too many, which would create a cycle.
This adds the counts instead of subtracting the components.

stem: A forest (a graph with no cycles) has \( [[n]] \) vertices and \( [[e]] \) edges. Into how many connected components is it split?
solution: Each edge added to a forest of isolated vertices merges two components into one, reducing the component count by 1. Starting from \( [[n]] \) isolated vertices, adding \( [[e]] \) edges (each merging two components, since a forest has no cycles) leaves \( [[n]] - [[e]] = [[answer]] \) components.
distractor_rationales: This has the subtraction backwards, giving a negative number of components.
This adds the edges to the vertices instead of subtracting them.
This ignores the edges entirely, as if none were present.
This mistakes the edge count itself for the number of components.
This is off by one from the correct number of components.
This is off by one from the correct number of components.

stem: A graph currently consists of \( [[c]] \) separate connected components. What is the MINIMUM number of edges that must be added (each edge joining two different components) to make the whole graph connected?
solution: Each added edge that joins two different components merges them into one, reducing the number of components by exactly 1. To go from \( [[c]] \) components down to 1, the minimum number of edges needed is \( [[c]] - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the component count directly, forgetting that the last edge already leaves the graph connected.
This adds one too many edges beyond what is needed.
This is the number of edges to join EVERY pair of components directly, far more than the minimum needed.
This double counts the merges required.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.

stem: What is the minimum possible number of edges in a connected (simple) graph on \( [[n]] \) vertices?
solution: The fewest edges that can keep \( [[n]] \) vertices connected occur exactly when the graph is a tree (connected with no extra, cycle-creating edges), and a tree on \( [[n]] \) vertices has \( [[n]] - 1 \) edges. So the minimum is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the number of vertices, one more edge than the minimum tree requires.
This adds an extra edge, which would create a cycle and is not needed for connectivity.
This is the MAXIMUM possible number of edges (a complete graph), not the minimum.
This roughly halves the vertex count, far below what is needed to stay connected.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
